title: The Anatomy of Story — 22 Steps
author: John Truby
source: The Anatomy of Story: 22 Steps to Becoming a Master Storyteller, 2007.

lineage.note: The deliberate anti-template. 22 steps organized around the character's MORAL and PSYCHOLOGICAL NEED, not act breaks. Structure should grow organically from the protagonist's desire line and moral argument; rigid 3-act counting kills stories.

stages.name: Self-revelation, need, and desire
stages.function: What the character will learn and the surface goal. Plant these first.

stages.name: Ghost and story world
stages.function: The past wound (ghost) and the present-day world that embodies it.

stages.name: Weakness and need
stages.function: Psychological and moral weaknesses. Moral need = a wrong the character does to others.

stages.name: Inciting event
stages.function: The event that disrupts and forces the desire to crystallize.

stages.name: Desire
stages.function: The specific, concrete goal the protagonist now pursues.

stages.name: Ally or allies
stages.function: Companions; foils that highlight the protagonist's traits.

stages.name: Opponent and/or mystery
stages.function: A figure (or system) competing for the same goal.

stages.name: Fake-ally opponent
stages.function: A character who appears to help but secretly opposes.

stages.name: First revelation and decision
stages.function: New information forces a changed plan.

stages.name: Plan
stages.function: The strategy the protagonist will execute against the opponent.

stages.name: Opponent's plan and main counterattack
stages.function: Antagonist's strategy is revealed; first major counterattack.

stages.name: Drive
stages.function: Series of actions in pursuit of the goal; protagonist may become increasingly immoral.

stages.name: Attack by ally
stages.function: An ally calls out the protagonist's flaws.

stages.name: Apparent defeat
stages.function: Major loss — appears unrecoverable.

stages.name: Second revelation and decision
stages.function: New insight; obsessive drive replaces or transforms the original desire.

stages.name: Audience revelation
stages.function: Audience learns something the protagonist doesn't yet know.

stages.name: Third revelation and decision
stages.function: Another insight forces another plan change.

stages.name: Gate, gauntlet, visit to death
stages.function: Crossing into the climactic space; metaphorical death-encounter.

stages.name: Battle
stages.function: Climactic confrontation.

stages.name: Self-revelation
stages.function: Protagonist sees their own moral need clearly — the heart of the story.

stages.name: Moral decision
stages.function: Acting on the self-revelation; the character chooses rightly under pressure.

stages.name: New equilibrium
stages.function: Aftermath; new state expresses the protagonist's transformation.

applicability.limitations: 22 steps is too many for routine assembly. Best used as a DIAGNOSTIC checklist: is the moral need present? Is there a self-revelation moment? Has the protagonist's desire transformed? Not as a slot-filling template.
